title: Tool repair triage board
category: Anatomy · Biology · Medicine · Tools · Repair · Construction
summary: A simple system to decide which tools get repaired first when time, parts, light, and energy are limited.
content: In a disruption, a broken tool can block water, heat, shelter, food, or medical work. A repair board prevents wasting rare parts on low-value fixes while critical failures wait. List each tool, its purpose, failure, needed part, risk if unfixed, person responsible, and next check. Repair first what protects life, water, shelter, sanitation, communication, and safe movement. Delay cosmetic repairs and duplicates. Keep broken parts, screws, washers, handles, blades, cords, and containers sorted so they can become spares later.
steps: List every broken or weak tool.
Mark whether it supports life, water, shelter, sanitation, food, communication, or movement.
Estimate whether repair is simple, uncertain, or dangerous.
Use parts first on tools with the highest safety impact.
Record temporary fixes so nobody trusts them like permanent repairs.
warnings: Do not repair electrical or pressure equipment beyond your skill level.
Do not use cracked handles, unstable ladders, or damaged cutting tools as if safe.
common_mistakes: Treating Tool repair triage board as finished after the first attempt instead of writing the next review time.
Moving people or supplies for Tool repair triage board without checking vulnerable people, exits, and safe limits first.
Mixing clean and unsafe items during Tool repair triage board because labels, dates, or responsible names were not written down.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Tool repair triage board with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Tool repair triage board if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Tool repair triage board and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Tool repair triage board or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Tool repair triage board are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.
The routine ignores non-diagnostic care, warning signs, records, and referral to qualified help and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Tool repair triage board”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Tool repair triage board as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Tool repair triage board when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Tool repair triage board.
short_term: For the first hours of Tool repair triage board, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Tool repair triage board remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Tool repair triage board fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Tool repair triage board to the real environment: check non-diagnostic care, warning signs, records, and referral to qualified help, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
